SAN FRANCISCO
wasHrNGroN. o.c-
LAW OFFICES OF
PILLSBURY MADISON & SUTRO
5U rTE 335
455 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAM ENTO. CALIFORN IA 954I4
TELEPHONE (9r6) 44r OA6a
TELECOPTER (9r6) 44r-3s63
ORANGE COUNTY
May 21 , L99t
Gift Rules under the Ethics
Act
Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair PoliticaL Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
This l-etter is a request for formal- written advice
pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b).
with the adoption by the electorate of Proposition
112 and the approval of Senate Bill 1738 and Assembly Bill
4143 by the Legislature and Governor, the gift and honoraria
limitations of Proposition 73 have been substantially
revised. As a result, several questions of interpretation
have arisen:
I. 
GIFTS FROM MULTIPLE DONORS.
Under FPPC Regulation L8726.6, a gift received
from multiple donors must be disclosed if the gift's 
value
equals or exceeds $50. Lobbyist employers which make such
gifts in participation with other donors must disclose the
ful-1 value of the gift to the recipient as an activity
expense, their total cost in connection with the gj-ft, and,
in a footnote, indicate the total cost of the activity and
the portion of the acti-vity attributable to the lobbyist
employer. See 2 Cal.Code Regs. S 18515(d); 1990 Information
Manua1 on Lobbying Disclosure Provisions of the Political
Reform Act, p. 50. We note that with the passage of the
gift and honoraria limitation of Proposition 73 (Gov't Code
S 85400) and the accompanying regulations (2 Cal-.Code Regs.
SS l-8531.5 and 18540), the Commission did not address the
allocation of the value of gifts made by multiple donors for
Proposition 73 gift limitation purposes.
Effective January L, 1-991, elected state officers,
members of state boards and commissions and designated
employees of state agencies are prohibited from accepting
gifts with a total value of more than $250 in any calendar
10s38038


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
\lay 2L, L99l
Page 2
year from a single source. Gov't Code SS 89504(a) and
89505(a). Furthermore. elected state officers will be
prohibited from accepting any honoraria. Gov't Code
S 89s02(a).
With these new restrictions in place, we seek
confirmation that if a gift is made to an elected state
officer, 
member of a state board or commission or designated
employee of a state agency by multiple donors, the vaLue of
the gj-ft for limitation purposes is the pro-rata share of
the gift paid for by the respective donors. For example. J_f
two donors each contribute fifty percent of the cost of a
$300 gift to a legislator, the legislator woul_d be deemed to
have received a gift equal to $150 from each source.
notwithstanding the information reported on the legislator,s
statement of economic interests and the donors' lobbyist
employer reports.
rI. 
MEANING OF "SINGLE SOURCE".
As noted above, the new gift limitations restrict
gifts made by a "single source." cov't Code SS 89504(a) and
89505(a). This phrase is not defined in SB 1738, nor is it
defined elsewhere in the Po1itical Reform Act. Although the
gift and honoraria Limitation of Proposition 73 (Gov,t Code
S 85400) used the same phrase, this section is expressly
repealed by the Ethics Act. SB 1738, S 3.5. In addition,
Judge Karlton's decision invalidated Government Code
sections 85301, 85302 and 85303(a) and (b). Service
l-990) ("SEIU"). Accordingly,
1990) ("SEIU"). Accordingly, the cited references for the
recently effective affiliation 
regulation (S 18531.5) have
(S 18531.s) have
either been repealed (S 85400), ruled unconstitutional (S
85301, 85302 and 85303(a) and (b)) or are threatened with
Iegal challenge (S 85305).
We therefore ask s
1.
Are the affiliation 
rules in Regulation
L8531.5 valid?
Do the Kahn (2 FPPC Ops. 151) and Lumsdon (2
FPPC Ops. L40) opinions continue to properly
reflect the Commission,s policy regarding
affiliation?
What is the meaning of the phrase ,,single
sourcerr as used in the gift linitation
provisions of the Ethics Act?
)
3.
747 F.Supp. 580
Sept. 25,


Scott Eallabrin. Esq.
May 21, 1991
Page 3
III. 
MEANING OF DISOUAIIFICATION PROVISIONS.
As discussed earJ.ier, SB 1738 and AB 4143 extend
penalties for violation of the Act's conflict of interest
provisions to actions of legislators and elected state
officers. 
Gov't Code SS 87102.5 and 87102.8. These are
intended to implement various provisions of Proposition 112.
See Cal.Const. art TV, S 4(a); art V, SS 5(d) and (f), 14(d)
and (f ). Although these constitutional contmands are of
parallel construction, the inplementing provisions in SB
L738 and AB 4L43 contain a significant, and confusing,
difference.
SB 1738 provides that governmental decisions which
may require disqualification of a legj-slator include, among
other provisions, the following:
1. 
Any action before the Legislature where g!!
of the following occur:
a. 
The legislator has received earned income
within the preceding L2 months from a
lobbyist employer;
b. 
The officer knows or has reason to know the
action or decision will have a direct and
significant financial impact on the lobbyist
employer; and
c. 
The action or decision wil-1 not have an
impact on the public generally or a signifi-
cant segment of the public in a sirniLar
manner. Gov't Code S 87102.5(a)(6).
2. 
Any action or decision before the Legislature
on legislation that the legislator knows or
has reason to know will have a direct and
significant impact on any person,
distinguishable from its impact on the public
generally or a significant segrment of the
public, from whom the legislator has received
any compensation within the preceding 12
months for the purpose of appearing, agreeing
to appear or taking any other action on
behalf of that person, before any locaI board
or agency. Gov't Code $ 87L02.5(a)(7).
AB 4143 adopted a much more limited scheme for the
disqualification of elected state officers (other than
Iegislators). 
Applicable decisions include variations of
only those cited above, rather than the full complement of
10538038


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
May 21, 1991
Page 4
decisions applicable to legislators. 
Compare Gov't Code
SS 87102.8(b) and 87102.5(a). However, the scheme for
elected state officers provides that an elected state
officer has a financial interest only when all of the
following occur:
a. 
The officer has received earned income within
the preceding 12 months from a lobbyist
employer;
b. 
The officer knows or has reason to know the
action or decision will have a direct and
significant financial impact on the lobbyj-st
emPloYer; and
c. 
The officer knows or has reason to know the
action or decision will have a direct and
significant imPact on any Person,
diltinguishable from its impact on the pubJ-ic
generally or a significant seg"ment of the.
public, irom whom the legislator has received
any comPensation within the preceding 12
months for the purpose of appearing, agreeing
to appear or taking any other action on
behalf of that person, before any local board
or agency. Gov't Code S 87102.8(b)(1)-(3).
In other words, for disqualification to result the
officer must receive earned incorne from a lobbyist employer
which will have the requisite financial impact on the
Iobbyist employer and the officer must receive compensation
fron a person for appearing before a 1oca1 board or agency.
Please confirm this analYsis.
Moreover. the provisions applicable to legislators
incorporate the definition of "financial interest" in the
Political Reform Act. while those affecting elected state
officers do not. 
Compare Gov't Code SS 87102.5(b)(2),
87L02.8 and 87103. This apparently means that despite the
adoption of Government Code section 87!02.8, elected state
officers are not required to aggregate the value of gifts
provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
iwithin 1,2 months Prior to the time when the decision is
made." Gov't Code S 87103(e). Please confirm this
analysis.
10538038


Scott Eallabrin, Esq.
May 21, J,991
Page 5
IV. 
INTERACTION OF DISOUAIIFICATION PROVISIONS
AND GIFT LIMITATIONS .
Under the Ethics Act, elected state officers
(including legislators), 
memlcers of state boards and
commj-ssions, and designated employees of state agencies,
generally will be prohibited from accepting gifts with a
total value of more than $250 in any calendar year from a
single source. Gov't Code SS 89504(c); 89505(a).
Legislators, members of state boards and commissions and
designated empJ.oyees of state agencies will be subject to
disqualification if they receive gifts with an aggregate
value of $250 "within 1.2 months prior to the time when the
decision is made." Gov't Code S 87103(e).
Assume a person gives a member of a state board a
gift with a value of $150 on November 15, 1991 and another
such gift with a value of $150 on ,fanuary 20, 7992. Assume
further that a governmental decision wil-l be rnade on
February 1, 1992 affecting the maker of the gifts above. ft
appears that, absent the making of any other gifts to the
sime official during calendar years 1990 and 1991, the gift
Iimitations have not been violated, but the board member may
be subject to disqualification since gifts aggregating over
$250 have been made within the 1.2 months of a decision.
Please confirm this anal.Ysis.
V. 
PROVISION OF INTRASTATE TRAVEL.
Under current FPPC regulations, reimbursements and
advances for actual intrastate travel and for necessary
accommodations provided directly in connection with a
speech, panel participation, seminar or similar event are
not gitt- or income and are not reportable by the recipient
official 
or the donor. 2 Cal.Code Regs. SS 18523(a),
L8728(a). In addition, travel and reasonable subsistence
provided in connection with a speech or. similar aPpearance
lre not subject to the honoraria prohibition. 
Gov't Code S
89s01.
SB 1738, in addition to repealing section 85400,
provides that payments, advances or reinbursements for
travel, including actual- transportation, lodging and
subsistence. will not be subject to the gift or honoraria
restrictions of the Ethics Act provided certain criteria are
met. See Gov't Code S 89505. In Iight of this legislative
change, is the intrastate/interstate travel- distinction
contiined in Regulations 18623 and L8728 still 
valid for
purposes of the Ethics Act?
10s38038


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
May 2L, !99L
Page 6
We appreciate your attention to this matter- If
you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact
me.
Very truly yours,
{*rV-- / ,\ft^*"/t"^--
Kathryn E. Donovan
cc: Ms. T. C. Lahaderne
Mr. F. K. Lowell
10538038

sections.question:
I. GIFTS FROM MULTIPLE DONORS.
Under FPPC Regulation 18726.6, a gift received from multiple donors must be disclosed if the gift's value equals or exceeds $50. Lobbyist employers which make such gifts in participation with other donors must disclose the full value of the gift to the recipient as an activity expense, their total cost in connection with the gift, and, in a footnote, indicate the total cost of the activity and the portion of the activity attributable to the lobbyist employer. See 2 Cal.Code Regs. S 18515(d); 1990 Information Manual on Lobbying Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act, p. 50. We note that with the passage of the gift and honoraria limitation of Proposition 73 (Gov't Code S 85400) and the accompanying regulations (2 Cal.Code Regs. SS 1-8531.5 and 18540), the Commission did not address the allocation of the value of gifts made by multiple donors for Proposition 73 gift limitation purposes.

II. MEANING OF "SINGLE SOURCE".
As noted above, the new gift limitations restrict gifts made by a "single source." Gov't Code SS 89504(a) and 89505(a). This phrase is not defined in SB 1738, nor is it defined elsewhere in the Political Reform Act.

III. MEANING OF DISQUALIFICATION PROVISIONS.
As discussed earlier, SB 1738 and AB 4143 extend penalties for violation of the Act's conflict of interest provisions to actions of legislators and elected state officers. Gov't Code SS 87102.5 and 87102.8.

IV. INTERACTION OF DISQUALIFICATION PROVISIONS AND GIFT LIMITATIONS.
Under the Ethics Act, elected state officers (including legislators), members of state boards and commissions, and designated employees of state agencies, generally will be prohibited from accepting gifts with a total value of more than $250 in any calendar year from a single source.

V. PROVISION OF INTRASTATE TRAVEL.
Under current FPPC regulations, reimbursements and advances for actual intrastate travel and for necessary accommodations provided directly in connection with a speech, panel participation, seminar or similar event are not gift or income and are not reportable by the recipient official or the donor.

contiined in Regulations 18623 and L8728 still 
valid for
purposes of the Ethics Act?
10s38038


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
May 2L, !99L
We appreciate your attention to this matter- If
you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact
me.
sections.question_synthetic: This letter requests formal written advice on five complex issues regarding California's new gift and honoraria limitations under Proposition 112, SB 1738, and AB 4143: (1) how to allocate gift values when multiple donors contribute to a single gift; (2) the definition of "single source" for gift limitation purposes; (3) the interpretation of disqualification provisions for legislators versus elected state officers; (4) the interaction between disqualification provisions and gift limitations; and (5) whether the intrastate/interstate travel distinction remains valid under the new Ethics Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response letter. The document contains a detailed QUESTION section but no CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section. The letter addresses five distinct topics (I-V) rather than a single question. OCR quality is generally good with minor formatting artifacts. Document references Government Code sections, FPPC regulations, and relevant legislation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I. GIFTS FROM MULTIPLE DONORS.
Under FPPC Regulation 18726.6, a gift received from multiple donors must be disclosed if the gift's value equals or exceeds $50. Lobbyist employers which make such gifts in participation with other donors must disclose the full value of the gift to the recipient as an activity expense, their total cost in connection with the gift, and, in a footnote, indicate the total cost of the activity and the portion of the activity attributable to the lobbyist employer. See 2 Cal.Code Regs. S 18515(d); 1990 Information Manual on Lobbying Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act, p. 50. We note that with the passage of the gift and honoraria limitation of Proposition 73 (Gov't Code S 85400) and the accompanying regulations (2 Cal.Code Regs. SS 1-8531.5 and 18540), the Commission did not address the allocation of the value of gifts made by multiple donors for Proposition 73 gift limitation purposes.

II. MEANING OF "SINGLE SOURCE".
As noted above, the new gift limitations restrict gifts made by a "single source." Gov't Code SS 89504(a) and 89505(a). This phrase is not defined in SB 1738, nor is it defined elsewhere in the Political Reform Act.

III. MEANING OF DISQUALIFICATION PROVISIONS.
As discussed earlier, SB 1738 and AB 4143 extend penalties for violation of the Act's conflict of interest provisions to actions of legislators and elected state officers. Gov't Code SS 87102.5 and 87102.8.

IV. INTERACTION OF DISQUALIFICATION PROVISIONS AND GIFT LIMITATIONS.
Under the Ethics Act, elected state officers (including legislators), members of state boards and commissions, and designated employees of state agencies, generally will be prohibited from accepting gifts with a total value of more than $250 in any calendar year from a single source.

V. PROVISION OF INTRASTATE TRAVEL.
Under current FPPC regulations, reimbursements and advances for actual intrastate travel and for necessary accommodations provided directly in connection with a speech, panel participation, seminar or similar event are not gift or income and are not reportable by the recipient official or the donor.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from Pillsbury Madison & Sutro law firm to the FPPC seeking formal written advice on five complex questions regarding the interpretation and application of California's newly enacted gift and honoraria limitations under Proposition 112, SB 1738, and AB 4143.
